title: Determinants and Limits of LLM Security-Tool Orchestration: A Study with HexStrike-AI
author: Gerard, Romain, Zeghaider, Assmaa, Guo, Yan
summary: This paper investigates the capabilities and limitations of LLM-based security-tool orchestrators, specifically studying HexStrike-AI with 150+ tools accessed via Model Context Protocol. Using 774 trials across picoCTF challenges, the authors identify that the client implementation is a first-order factor (2.1× performance gap between DeepSeek clients), and demonstrate that targeted improvements increase solve rate from 55.4% to 72.0%. Residual failures stem from reasoning limitations rather than missing tools. The work provides empirical insights into LLM agent evaluation methodology and the interplay between model, orchestrator design, and tool availability in security automation.
content: Computer Science > Software Engineering arXiv:2607.02873 (cs) [Submitted on 3 Jul 2026] Title: Determinants and Limits of LLM Security-Tool Orchestration: A Study with HexStrike-AI Authors: Romain Gerard , Assmaa Zeghaider , Yan Guo View a PDF of the paper titled Determinants and Limits of LLM Security-Tool Orchestration: A Study with HexStrike-AI, by Romain Gerard and 1 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: Large language model agents driving security tool suites over the Model Context Protocol are increasingly common. Yet the factors that bound their capability remain poorly characterized: how much depends on the model versus the client that drives it, whether constraining the agent to the orchestrator's own tools helps, and where capability is limited by reasoning rather than by missing tools. Using HexStrikeAI, an open-source orchestrator that exposes 150+ tools, as a testbed, we follow a methodology that evaluates the system, diagnoses its failures, and applies targeted improvements. We run 86 picoCTF challenges across seven categories and three difficulty tiers, under three tool-access regimes and three model/client configurations (774 trials). We then apply corrections to existing tools, agent-behavior changes, and eleven new capability tools, and re-run the previously-unsuccessful trials. The diagnosis isolates the driving client as a first-order factor for a fixed model (a 2.1 * gap between two DeepSeek clients) and a monotonic difficulty gradient, with the largest gains in the mid tier. The overall solve rate rises from 55.4% to 72.0%, and every configuration improves significantly (paired McNemar p < 0.001, non-overlapping 95% confidence intervals). The residual failures are reasoning- or environment-bound rather than missing-tool. A 60-run stability sub-study finds single-run verdicts reproducible (17/20 unanimous). We discuss what the results imply for how such orchestrators should be evaluated, and we are explicit about the limits: the study uses a single benchmark, the fixes were tuned on the same challenges they were evaluated on, and the client effect is demonstrated for one model only, so its generality to other models remains a hypothesis. takeaways: Client implementation is primary performance determinant (2.1× gap), exceeding tool-access regime effects.
Solve rate improves from 55.4% to 72.0% via targeted tool fixes and agent behavior changes.
Residual failures are reasoning- or environment-bound, not tool-availability problems.
Single-run verdicts highly reproducible (17/20 unanimous in stability study).
Improvements plateau at mid-tier difficulty; largest gains in moderate challenge range.

title: Agentic Engineering
summary: This article presents core design patterns for building autonomous software agents in 2026. It establishes agentic engineering as a discipline focused on architecting systems where agents act independently within defined constraints, emphasizing reliability, observability, and composability. The piece matters for technical practitioners because agent-based systems are becoming production-critical; understanding established patterns prevents costly architectural mistakes and enables teams to build scalable, trustworthy autonomous systems that can operate with minimal human intervention while maintaining predictability and auditability.
takeaways: Agent design requires explicit constraint definition and permission boundaries to operate reliably at scale without continuous supervision.
Observability and logging are foundational—agents must expose their reasoning, decisions, and state changes for debugging and compliance auditing.
Composable agent patterns enable reusable, modular autonomous components that integrate cleanly with existing software infrastructure.
Fallback mechanisms and graceful degradation are critical; agents must fail safely and escalate to humans when confidence thresholds drop.

title: Evaluation Guidebook - a Hugging Face Space by OpenEvals
summary: This appears to be a brief reference to a Hugging Face Space called 'Evaluation Guidebook' by OpenEvals, focused on agent evaluation methods. The entry is extremely minimal with only the word 'Refreshing' as content, providing no substantive information about methodology, findings, or technical depth. Without fuller content, it is unclear what specific evaluation approaches or frameworks are presented, limiting its utility as a comprehensive resource.
takeaways: OpenEvals maintains an Evaluation Guidebook resource on Hugging Face Spaces
Focus area involves agent evaluation methodologies
Limited information provided in source material
